title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fired boss is back with a vengeance By Lynne Curry Management (Published: November 25, 2002) Q. Several months ago, our store manager fired my supervisor for stealing and appointed me assistant manager. I've worked here for a year and didn't realize that by accepting this promotion I put my future at risk. Last week my manager told me he planned to leave the company. He said he was sorry he didn't give me more warning but he had finally "had it up to here" with corporate and was going to take some time off. He also told me that I was the best employee he had ever supervised. After talking with me, he cleaned out his desk and left. Thirty minutes later, my former supervisor walked in the door. She greeted me and told me that she was the new manager. I was stunned. She then told me that …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Santa makes a call to young earthquake victims SHOOK UP: Visit gives village children a respite from aftershocks. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 23, 2002) Fun can take the edge off a shaky situation: That's the philosophy of Kenny Timberwolf Gardner, who sent Santa Claus to Tanacross on Friday to cheer up children caught in the heart of the Nov. 3 earthquake. "Certainly my ancestors would have taken care of the people and their needs. Following in my ancestors' footsteps, it's for me to try to do it in the 21st century -- to bridge our culture into the things that will help people today," he said in a phone interview Thursday from his home, where he was packing toys to take north to the quake zone. The earthquake awoke Gardner's "warrior" instinct, when he f…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Campaign sign burner is off the hook CANCELED: Case closed; DA's office cites 'discretionary reasons.' By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 22, 2002) The district attorney's office has declined to prosecute a man who stood on a sidewalk last month and burned a Murkowski-for-governor campaign sign. Patrick Blomquist, 26, had been charged by police with criminally negligent burning, a Class A misdemeanor that carries a fine of up to $5,000 and a jail term of up to one year. The case has been canceled, said Heidi Peters, a spokeswoman for the district attorney. Peters cited "discretionary reasons" but did not elaborate. Blomquist filed a complaint with the Anchorage Police Department, saying that the officer who initially arrested him used unnecessary roughness and mad…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: November 22, 2002) FAIRBANKS Salcha floodwaters recede as Boondox Bar ice jam loosens The flooding in Salcha caused by an ice jam in the Tanana River has receded. The National Weather Service called off its flood warning about 10 a.m. Wednesday. "It appears the ice jam had loosened a bit and some of the water has gone downstream," meteorologist Ed Plumb told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. The roughly mile-long, 200-foot-wide jam in the Boondox Bar area remained and was likely to freeze in place, said Tim Biggane, director of emergency operations for the Fairbanks North Star Borough. Biggane has been monitoring the situation, along with Salcha Rescue, since it began Tuesday and will continue to watch the ice until freezeup. At its height, the jam dammed the ri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Transition shakes up state jobs TURNOVER: As many as 700 employees can be removed at any time. By Bill Mcallister Daily News Correspondent (Published: November 18, 2002) Juneau -- In state government, one person's transition can be someone else's layoff. As the outgoing administration of Democratic Gov. Tony Knowles yields to the incoming administration of Republican Gov.-elect Frank Murkowski, hundreds of jobs hang in the balance. There are about 700 executive branch employees without civil service protection who can be removed at any time, according to Commissioner of Administration Jim Duncan. But, he added, "no administration has ever gone to that depth." The governor's Cabinet -- commissioners of the 14 principal agencies -- probably will turn over completely after the Dec. 2 inau…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Bill would help Native corporations EXCHANGE: Swap gives Cape Fox Corp., Sealaska 12,000 acres of land. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 21, 2002) Two Alaska Native corporations would be the big winners from a controversial land exchange that cleared the U.S. Senate on Tuesday. Under the bill, sponsored by Sen. Frank Murkowski, R-Alaska, Cape Fox Corp. of Ketchikan and Juneau-based Sealaska Corp. would receive up to 12,000 acres of Forest Service land, mostly near Berner's Bay, in exchange for giving up mineral rights and second-growth forest elsewhere in Southeast. The bill requires House approval before year-end. Situated about 40 miles north of downtown Juneau, Berner's Bay is heavily used by hunters, hikers, fishermen and kayakers. It's also adjacent to the…

title: Search Alaska articles for...
summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Wife's death city's 18th slaying By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 29, 2002) A 46-year-old woman was shot at a Spenard gas station early Monday, allegedly by her estranged husband, who then turned the gun on himself in a suicide attempt, police said. Gail D. Dorr was gunned down in the parking lot of 2-Go-Tesoro, 4608 Spenard Road, shortly before 5 a.m., police said. She was pronounced dead at Alaska Native Medical Center. Dorr's husband, Robert R. Dorr, 51, was taken to Alaska Native Medical Center in critical condition. Police spokesman Ron McGee would not describe Robert Dorr's wounds, and a hospital nursing supervisor would not comment on his condition. No one was immediately charged. The Do…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kenai gas to run out in 10 years REPORT: Pipeline, other sources needed or economy would suffer. The Associated Press (Published: November 20, 2002) Kenai -- A new report spells out a bleak future for Cook Inlet natural gas. The analysis by Kenai Peninsula Borough staff predicts the known reserves of natural gas in the Cook Inlet Basin will last about 10 years. And unless more gas is found here or piped in from elsewhere, the Kenai Peninsula Borough economy could suffer. The analysis of current trends in the oil and gas industry was compiled from several industry, state and federal sources and delivered in a report to the borough assembly by Bill Popp, the borough's oil and gas liaison. Popp, an assembly member until early July, was hired by the borough primarily to help promote a natu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Native groups launch minority bone marrow drive NEED: American Indians, Alaska Natives have less chance of finding donors. The Associated Press (Published: November 8, 2002) Kenai -- Several Kenai Peninsula organizations are addressing a serious shortage of Alaska Native and American Indian bone marrow donations. The Kenaitze Indian Tribe, Kenaitze Cuya Qyut'anen Head Start, Salamatof Native Association and the Blood Bank of Alaska Kenai Peninsula Center are teaming up to host a minority bone marrow testing and blood drive next week. The drive will be from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. Nov. 15 at the Family House in Kenai. Kenaitze Head Start director Connie Wirz said there is a shortage of bone marrow donations from Native people because they have not been informed of its need. "It is an awarenes…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | BP agrees to Prudhoe Bay spill settlement CLEANUP: Oil firm will pay $675,000 for last year's accident. The Associated Press Wire (Published: November 16, 2002) BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. has agreed to pay $675,000 to the state to settle claims and cleanup monitoring costs from a pipeline leak on the North Slope last year that spilled crude oil and methanol. While BP says the spill was 10,000 gallons, the state Department of Environmental Conservation says it may have been as large as 60,000 gallons. Whatever the amount, BP already has spent more than $2 million cleaning it up, according to company spokesman Paul Laird. "We don't believe there was any long-term environmental damage," Laird said. State officials say the harm was minimal. The spill occurred after a valve was inadverten…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: November 14, 2002) TRF managing Frontier Building TRF Management Corp. has taken over management of the Frontier Building, a large Midtown Anchorage office building at 36th Avenue and B Street. Bob Parks co-founded TRF in 1980 to develop Northway Mall and later the Frontier Building. He sold the company in 1999 but reconstituted the company to take over the Frontier on Oct. 1, according to TRF. The company manages other buildings in the Pacific Northwest, TRF said. Jim Wood will remain manager of the Frontier, the company said. Land parcels to be auctioned The Alaska Department of Natural Resources is selling close to 2,000 state-owned parcels of land. The property is offered through a sealed bid auction. Bids must be received by Nov. 22. Parcels are …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Loggers fell stream buffer law SHEARED: Punishment ranges from warning to $10,000 fine. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 20, 2002) Loggers working for Afognak Native Corp. razed the forested banks of a silver salmon stream in violation of Alaska's forestry laws, state officials said. It's unclear if what happened along the roughly 300-foot reach was an accident or whether the cutters knew the narrow, twisty stream contained salmon and flouted the law, said state habitat biologist David Ryland, who inspected the site on Nov. 6. Ryland said he got conflicting stories from the loggers. Under the Alaska Forest Practices Act, loggers must leave 66-foot-wide tree buffers on either side of a salmon stream on state and private land. On federal land, the minimum width i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Teacher faces sex charges HERITAGE: Christian school instructor, a 'nice guy', fired and jailed. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 20, 2002) Police have arrested and charged a popular Heritage Christian School teacher with three counts of sexual abuse of a minor stemming from a relationship he had with a student. Brion Williams, 28, turned himself in late Tuesday and was taken to Anchorage Jail, police said. Williams, athletic director at the South Anchorage private school, was fired Monday after he admitted having a sexual relationship with a 17-year-old female student, said Lynn Stowe, the school's chief administrator. The contact apparently started last summer and ended in September, Stowe said. It's illegal in Alaska for a teacher or anyone in an authori…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Two Alaska educators win Milken awards The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(November 1, 8:05 a.m. AST) - Two Alaska educators have been honored with National Educator Awards from the Milken Family Foundation of Los Angeles. The awards carry a prize of $25,000 for each recipient. Sheila Wallace, a teacher at the Ayaprun Elitnaurvik charter school in Bethel and Susan Okeson, the Principal at Wonder Park Elementary School in Anchorage were notified Thursday that they had won. Educators are nominated for the award without their knowledge by a panel appointed by the state department of education. Wallace is a graduate of the Lower Kuskokwim School District and the University of Alaska Fairbanks. She has been a teacher since 1994. Okeson has been an elementary school principal for four years. She …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crimestoppers (Published: November 18, 2002) Information sought on house break-in Police need help investigating a break-in between July 12 and 18 of a residence on Elkins Drive in Wasilla. Someone caused about $5,000 in damage and took property valued at $27,800, including a 1972 brown Ford pickup and a 1981 black Harley-Davidson motorcycle. The pickup was recovered July 19 on a road off Edelweis. The motorcycle was found Sept. 18 in a ditch near the residence. Other stolen items include a Monitor oil stove, a Honda Trac snowblower, several power and hand tools, a set of welding torches and tanks, and a Zenith 25-inch television. If you have information about this or any other crime, call Mat-Su Crimestoppers at 1-907-745-3333. Your call could earn you up to $1,000 if the information …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Employee may open mail for co-worker By Lynne Curry (Published: November 18, 2002) Q. Our receptionist routinely opens and routes the managers' mail. Few of our other employees get much mail, but yesterday a letter came for an accounting clerk. The receptionist followed the same procedure she does with other mail and sliced the envelope open. When she realized the contents were personal, she routed the envelope directly to the clerk with a note that said, "Sorry, didn't read this, seemed personal." Well, the accounting clerk went ballistic. Apparently, she subscribes to a singles service and uses the office address for this mail, as she didn't want her teenage children to notice the return address. She stormed into the reception area, called the receptionist snoopy and perverted and sc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community profile (Published: November 14, 2002) Thorne Bay POPULATION: 557 (2000) LOCATION: On the east coast of Prince of Wales Island, 47 air miles northwest of Ketchikan, 60 miles from Hollis and 36 miles east of the Klawock Junction on the island road system. DESCRIPTION: Evolving from a work camp, Thorne Bay has become a year-round home to many logging employees, almost all of them non-Native. Employment is primarily related to the logging industry and federal management of the Tongass National Forest, with some commercial fishing, tourism and government employment. To supplement income, residents fish and trap. The 2000 U.S. Census found that 269 residents were employed, median household income was $45,625, per capita income was $20,836, and almost 8 percent of residents were li…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Elders teach kids to crochet, help others GLADYS WOOD: Students make hats, scarves for needy teens. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 16, 2002) The Gladys Wood Elementary student council is in the loop of community service with a project to make scarves and hats for homeless teens at Covenant House. "It feels good," is how Alisha Graham, the council's vice president, described spending the morning working on the project at the Anchorage Senior Center. "I only knew a little about crocheting," she said. "It's easy." The idea bridges generations, with elders teaching youngsters how to crochet and the beneficiaries being homeless and troubled teens. Instead of doing her regular silent reading activity Wednesday morning, Graham found herself sitting in the Senior Ce…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Passing the test Pipeline rocked and rolled, but stood up to the quake By Mary Pemberton The Associated Press (Published: November 10, 2002) The trans-Alaska oil pipeline was designed to withstand an 8.5 magnitude earthquake, but could it? When a team of structural and geo-technical engineers came up with the pipeline design in the early 1970s, they didn't expect it to be tested in their lifetimes. They were wrong. Sunday's 7.9 magnitude earthquake -- the worst ever recorded on the Denali fault in Alaska's Interior -- struck in a sparcely populated area 90 miles south of Fairbanks, the state's second largest city. The earthquake -- considered by seismologists as a once-every-600-years event -- left a 145-mile crack across the landscape and was the first significant quake to test the pi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cleanup of grounded vessel under way COST RISING: Agencies estimate $250,000 spent so far in removing pollutants. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 17, 2002) The cost of cleaning up the hulking part-scorched wreck of the 150-foot Genei Maru totaled roughly $250,000 by Saturday -- and is rising. A Japanese company owns the 140-ton squid catcher that suddenly appeared on the rocks at Afognak Island earlier this month after catching fire in May and disappearing in the North Pacific Ocean for months. But the cleanup money right now is coming out of the National Pollution Funds Center, a section of Superfund, said Marsha Delaney, a spokeswoman for the U.S. Coast Guard based in Kodiak. The agencies involved are keeping track of spending so they can bill the vessel's …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Student hurt in bus incident EPISODE: During dispute, girl falls, breaks her arm while vehicle was moving. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 14, 2002) A high school student broke her arm Wednesday afternoon when she apparently fell out of a school bus through the front door while the bus was in motion, an Anchorage School District official said. Schools district transportation director Steve Kalmes declined to identify the girl, a student at East High School. He also would not name the bus driver, saying the case was a personnel matter. The incident occurred about 2:30 p.m. at Bragaw Street and East Seventh Avenue after students on the bus, on their way home from East, became disruptive, Kalmes said. An Anchorage woman who called the newspaper said the injured st…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Groups seek protection for orcas PRINCE WILLIAM SOUND: Listing would draw attention to pollution. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 14, 2002) Fearing that industrial contaminants from across the sea have combined with other factors to drive a small, unique family of Prince William Sound killer whales toward extinction, conservation groups are asking government to list the animals as depleted under the Marine Mammal Protection Act. Over the past 13 years, the AT1 group appears to have lost 13 of 22 members in a decline never documented before among killer whales in the North Pacific. A depleted listing would require the National Marine Fisheries Service to figure out if something could be done to help the animals -- and would draw dramatic local attention to the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Thank yous (Published: November 14, 2002) SPONSORS APPRECIATED: Coach Steve Day of Anchorage writes: "The Cook Inlet Soccer Club Girls U-12 Flames would like to thank their generous corporate sponsors for making the 2002 outdoor season a championship season. Our appreciation is extended to: the Alaskan Long House Hotel, Hobbycraft Inc., Enstar Natural Gas and SKW/Eskimos Inc. We appreciate your support." DANCERS ENTHRALL: Mirror Lake Middle School thanks the Palabuniyan Kulintang Ensemble and the Anchorage School District Multicultural Education department for bringing Philippine dancers and musicians to the school for an all-school assembly. "Students were enthralled by the sounds and delighted to participate in the dances. This multicultural show helped enrich our curriculum and stud…

summary: | | Volunteer Opportunities (Published: November 14, 2002) The Volunteer Center is a community resource center sponsored by United Way of Anchorage. If you are interested in volunteering, call listings below or contact Sue Brogan at the Volunteer Center at 263-3821 or sbrogan@ak.org . For information online, see www.uway.ak.org . Taton was recently named Region IV Middle School Principal of the Year. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News ) Principal Glen Nielsen greets his bus-riding students by name as they arrive at Northwood Elementary School Friday morning in Spenard. summary: • Submit events/announcements • Check stock prices and news Embargo talk fuels ANWR bid DRILLING: GOP says U.S. vulnerable if Saddam cuts supply. (Published: April 9, 2002) Washington -- Warning that Iraqi President Saddam Hussein's decision to cut off his oil supply for 30 days may plunge the United States into gasoline shortages with long lines at the pump, Senate Republicans plan to press this week for approval of exploration in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Jury selection begins for pipeline shooting suspect TRIAL: Lewis' attorney worries that weapons conviction will taint jurors. The Associated Press (Published: November 14, 2002) Fairbanks -- Attorneys for both sides of the trial of Daniel Lewis, who is suspected of shooting the trans-Alaska oil pipeline, are concerned that the case may have been decided in the media. Jury selection began Tuesday. Nearly every person in a pool of about 70 potential jurors said they were familiar with the pipeline shooting through media coverage. But most of those excused said a scheduling conflict or personal involvement in the case -- not media coverage -- would prevent them from being jurors. Adam Gurewitz, one of Lewis' lawyers, said after Tuesday's proceedings that it was too early to know if an imp…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Fiscal gap dominates Anchorage debate The Associated Press (Published: October 24, 2002) ANCHORAGE(AP) - Both candidates in the race for governor were on the defensive Wednesday night as they again tackled the state's gap between revenue and spending in a televised debate. While Democrat Fran Ulmer dodged the pro-tax label put on her by Republican opponent Frank Murkowski, Murkowski speculated that unexplored oil prospects could yield huge amounts of new revenue to close the gap. "I don't have a tax plan. I have a parachute plan," Ulmer said at the live debate broadcast by KTUU Channel 2 from the Alaska Center for the Performing Arts. Ulmer explained that her tax proposal would only take effect if the state's reserves fal…

WINNER CREEK GORGE TRAIL: This 5.5-mile round-trip trails follows boardwalks and crosses several bridges across short sections of muskeg that dot the spruce and hemlock forest. It also features a hand-pulled tram spanning Glacier Creek on a cable. summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Solomon receives award By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 26, 2002) The Alaska Federation of Natives gave its highest award Friday to Jonathan Solomon, the Fort Yukon orator and traditional chief who is probably the pre-eminent Native leader to speak out against oil drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. Solomon, 70, received the citizen of the year award from the AFN, which cited his fight against oil drilling to protect the Porcupine caribou herd and the traditional way of life of the Gwich'in Indians. The AFN itself is on record in support of "environmentally sound development" of ANWR. The pro-drilling stance was approved by a divided convention in 1995 after an exhausting two-hour-plus d…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Lead levels halts shooting program HAZARD: High amounts of toxin are discovered in school rifle team. By Tim Mowry Fairbanks Daily News-Miner (Published: November 15, 2002) Fairbanks -- Youth shooting programs at the Tanana Valley Sportsmen's Association shooting range have been halted after 10 members of the Lathrop High School rifle team were found to have high concentrations of lead in their blood. Lead levels in the team members ranged from 17 to 37 micrograms per deciliter. The normal level is less than 10 micrograms in children. "These levels are very high," said state epidemiologist John Middaugh with the Division of Public Health. "They're way beyond what we expected to see." While the levels are not dangerous at this point, Middaugh recommended that junior shooting programs at…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kenai pushes for drilling in Katalla EXPLORATION: City officials urge nod for oil, gas venture. The Associated Press (Published: November 12, 2002) Kenai -- The Kenai City Council is urging federal approval of an exploratory drilling program in a remote location near the eastern side of Prince William Sound. City officials are hopeful the proposed venture in Katalla by Cassandra Energy Corp. of Kenai could directly benefit Kenai. Katalla, about 50 miles south of Cordova, is the site of Alaska's first commercial oil production, from 1902 to the early 1930s. Attempts at oil production on the site were abandoned until the 1980s, when exploration was again attempted, but not brought into production. Cassandra Energy, with its office in Anchorage, has started a permitting process to begin n…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crews take stock of wrecked ship GENEI MARU: Thousands of gallons of fuel said to be on board. The Associated Press (Published: November 14, 2002) Crews were beginning work Wednesday to remove fuel from a wrecked Japanese fishing vessel that washed up on Afognak Island last week. The 97-foot Genei Maru No. 7 was fishing for squid outside U.S. waters when it caught fire May 27 and was abandoned by its crew. It had been adrift since then before running aground at Afognak Island, 25 miles north of Kodiak. It was spotted Sunday. Coast Guard officials estimate the Genei Maru has about 20,000 gallons of diesel fuel on board. They are also concerned about the presence of hydrogen sulfide and methane gases on the vessel from debris and rotting squid. And they fear the ship's refrigeration syst…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: November 14, 2002) ANCHORAGE Police seek help in looking for missing 21-year-old Police are investigating the disappearance of Doug Foster, who vanished while celebrating his 21st birthday with friends. He was last seen Saturday, in the area of 12th Avenue and E Street, in downtown Anchorage. Foster may have been intoxicated, police said. Foster is 5 feet, 10 inches tall and weighs 155 pounds. He has black hair with red highlights and brown eyes. Anyone who has seen Foster or knows his whereabouts is asked to call Anchorage Police dispatch at 786-8900. -- Anchorage Daily News POINT HOPE Plan for cleaning contaminated sites available for review A plan for cleaning up contaminated sites at the Cape Lisburne Long Range Radar Site is up for public comment and a me…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Native village in charge of its own tourism destiny By JAMES MACPHERSON, The Associated Press JUNEAU(November 8, 5:50 p.m. AST) - Tourists making a port call at Metlakatla can count on dropping dollars in the Tsimshian Indian community in Southeast Alaska. The Annette Island town run by the Metlakatla Indian Community is the only reservation in Alaska, with its own courts and power to manage fish and game use. And unlike other destinations in Alaska where Outside tour companies often have a stake in everything from geegaws to glacier viewing, the village runs its own visitor industry. Once visitors cross a tour ship gangway, they are met by village-owned buses and taken to village-run events like Native dancing performances and art shows. Even walking tours come at a price. "When they …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | No more sales Law aims to phase out wolf hybrids in a generation Mary Pemberton The Associated Press (Published: November 11, 2002) Werner Shuster hugs Kojak, one of his wolf hybrids. Wolf-dog owners in Alaska are facing a new state regulation designed to phase out the hybrids in a generation, and Shuster says he plans to fight the regulation tooth and nail. ( Photo by Al Grillo / The Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge Palmer -- Werner Shuster's love affair with wolves began when he was a boy growing up in Yonkers, N.Y., and neighbors owned a timberwolf that became his best friend. His passion has grown into Wolf Country USA, where the 72-year-old Alaskan keeps 52 wolf hybrids on chains behind 8-foot stockade fencing at his 42-acre spread north of Anchorage. But Shuster and th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Village Aviation in for big haul REVAMPED: Pricey insurance prompts firm to switch to cargo. The Associated Press (Published: November 13, 2002) Anchorage -- Market changes and increased regulation of the airline industry forced Village Aviation Inc. owner Don King to make a tough decision for the future of his company. "We needed to become a cargo airline, a passenger airline, or no airline at all," King told the Alaska Journal of Commerce. Skyrocketing insurance rates nixed any plans for continued passenger service for the airline, King said. And folding the company meant putting hardworking employees out of work and losing the money he has invested. So the company went into the cargo business, King said. "We have good infrastructure, good people and good credit, so we're as healthy …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: November 12, 2002) WASILLA Notorious stretch of highway due for overhaul The state will spend $35 million over the next two years to straighten and widen a dangerous stretch of the Glenn Highway north of Palmer, Gov. Tony Knowles' office said Friday. The work will cover a stretch from Mile 100 to Mile 109 that includes the Caribou Creek canyon. The stretch of road, which dives steeply into the canyon, is notorious for accidents, especially ones involving tractor trailers. The work will begin next year and take two years to complete. The project includes adding wider shoulders and building retaining walls to rein in rock slides. -- Anchorage Daily News KETCHIKAN Board takes action against doctor's license The Alaska State Medical Board recently put Dr. Gena McK…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sorting Out The Senate New Leaders: Therriault Will Be Body's President; Green, Wilken Get Finance By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 8, 2002) Newly Named Senate President Gene Therriault Outlines Plans As New Senate Majority Leader Ben Stevens Listens During A Press Conference Thursday Afternoon At The Legislative Information Office Downtown. ( Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Republican state senators announced Thursday they have chosen Gene Therriault of North Pole as Senate president, two days after Alaska's voters guaranteed continued GOP control of the body. The Republican lawmakers gathered this week in Sen. John Cowdery's South Anchorage home to hash out who among them will lead the Senate for the next two years. Ther…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Bond votes will indicate confidence in economy By Tim Bradner Economy (Published: November 3, 2002) Alaskans go the polls Tuesday. Along with electing or defeating candidates for office, we'll make some powerful statements in our approval, or disapproval, of $465 million in proposed state general obligation bonds. For one thing, voters will express their confidence -- or lack of it -- in our state's economy through their willingness to incur debt. Approval of the bonds also carries with it an implied willingness to pay, for citizens to shoulder some costs for public services themselves rather than have state oil revenue pay for so much. Third, we'll register our level of concern for rural Alaskans, through proposals to rebuild many deteriorated rural schools. These bonds would pay for …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Payback time Teen learns expensive consequences of dial-around codes By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 1, 2002) When Palmer resident Bob Ham received a $450 long-distance phone bill from Alaska Communications Systems, he thought it was a mistake. After all, he didn't have an account with the company. He didn't even have a long-distance service on his phone. But the charges were all too real. A teenager in Ham's household had made calls using a dial-around number, a code to access long-distance service providers. "What happened was my friend told me about this code," said Martina Martella, 17. The word was "it's free long distance." So she started calling friends in Eagle River and Anchorage and family members in South Carolina. The "free long distance" rumor is…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Teacher helps shape policy FELLOWSHIP: Anne Pfitzner feeds education information to senators. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 4, 2002) This time of year, Anne Pfitzner normally would be seizing every spare second of snowless weather to cart her sixth-graders to Soldotna Creek for a firsthand look at moose tracks and juvenile silver salmon. Instead, the 32-year-old Soldotna Elementary teacher is up to her elbows in some heady, big-picture stuff. Pfitzner is more than a month into an influential fellowship in the nation's capital that allows her to tap the brightest minds in education. The teacher with a zest for science finds herself "trapped inside" a cubicle in the office of Sen. Joe Lieberman, D-Conn. Her role there is to help gather the best information on th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crewman plunges overboard LOST: Coast Guard calls off search for man tangled in fishing gear. Staff Reports (Published: November 5, 2002) The Bering Sea claimed another life Monday. Mike Moran, 35, apparently fell overboard after becoming entangled in fishing gear on the Western Queen, a 178-foot catcher-processor that was longlining for cod. Moran plummeted into the frigid ocean water wearing only a sweatshirt-type jacket, said U.S. Coast Guard Lt. j.g. Asheley Bodkin. He was not wearing a life vest or a survival suit, she said. The Western Queen was fishing about 15 miles south of St. George Island when the accident happened. The captain of the vessel made a mayday call around 7:50 a.m. A Coast Guard C-130 arrived about three hours later. Bodkin said the plane got there as fast as it…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Economy, Sept. 11 hit tourism ALASKA: Adventure travel firms, Interior hardest hit, report hints. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: October 10, 2002) Juneau -- Alaska's tourism industry weathered an overall lackluster summer as cruise ship passengers spent less and fewer independent travelers made the trip, according to a survey for the state's top travel association. About 1.2 million travelers came to Alaska this summer, the same number as the summer before, according to the survey for the Alaska Travel Industry Association. The preliminary findings of the survey conducted by the McDowell Group were presented at an association meeting in Juneau on Wednesday. It showed that cruise ship companies lured a record 720,000 passengers to Alaska with discounted fares and heav…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Solar-powered fence thwarts bears SMOKEHOUSE: The apparatus was installed as a model two years ago. By Erik Hillstrom Bristol Bay Times (Published: July 15, 2002) Dillingham -- While the sight of a brown bear in the wild can be awe-inspiring, the sight of a furry thief in your smokehouse filled with drying salmon can be downright discouraging. Just ask Anuska Olson. She and her husband, Hjalmar, have spent their entire lives in the Dillingham area, and too many times have unwillingly donated their family's fish to an ursine robber. "One year I had over 200 fish in the smokehouse," Anuska said. "I went and opened the door one day, and there was a big hole in the back of the smokehouse. All my fish were gone but about 20, and those were sour. That bear ate the good ones and left the sour…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Focus shifts in new wildlife plan GAME: Agency responds to increased federal funding for nontraditional areas. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 21, 2002) Alaskans more interested in shooting photographs than shooting animals are recognized in a new strategic plan that outlines the future of the game side of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. The plan says the Division of Wildlife Conservation should continue to focus its traditional efforts on game animals that are hunted and trapped but should expand its work in nontraditional areas, such as wildlife viewing, education and management of animals that aren't sought as game. "It is a change," said Doug Larsen, deputy director of the agency, but not because the division has been reluctant to emphasize so-called no…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Group works to make good on promises to kids SYMPOSIUM: Needs, wishes of local children will be discussed. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 20, 2002) What if every child were born healthy and had a safe place to go before and after school and a caring adult to talk to? What if all children had an education that prepared them for meaningful work and ways to make them feel like an important part of the larger community? In 1997, all of this nation's living presidents gathered at the Presidents' Summit for America's Future in Philadelphia and challenged communities to satisfy each of those needs for every child. The summit gave rise to America's Promise: The Alliance for Youth, founded by Gen. Colin Powell, and started a grass-roots national movement. State delegates t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | First Nation leader pushes pipeline The Associated Press (Published: October 9, 2002) Whitehorse, Yukon -- A Yukon First Nation leader says an Alaska Highway pipeline might could prove a boost to the territory, even it it's not likely to solve all the territory's economic woes. Kwanlin Dun Chief Rick O'Brien flew to Calgary Monday morning to begin a weeklong campaign in support of an Alaska Highway pipeline. With him were Darwin O'Brien, the Kwanlin Dun's councillor in economic development, and Mike Smith, negotiator for Kwanlin Dun land claims. The group will be meeting with industry and government officials throughout the next week in Calgary, Ottawa and Washington. Rick O'Brien noted that not everyone is against providing subsidies for a pipeline. "On the one hand federal ministers …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Foothill spurns offer to buy veneer plant AIDEA: Equipment used by Gateway is scheduled for auction. The Associated Press (Published: July 16, 2002) Ketchikan -- An auction to sell assets of a veneer plant was still on schedule Monday despite efforts by the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority to buy the business. Foothill Capital Corp. turned down an offer by AIDEA to purchase equipment used by now defunct Gateway Forest Products, AIDEA director Bob Poe said. But the state authority will continue efforts to stop the auction, which is scheduled for today. The Ketchikan Gateway Borough Assembly was also seeking to intervene in the sale. "We are still very engaged in this thing and are all very hopeful," Poe said. Gateway Forest Products filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy prot…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Southeast tourism spots designated PLAN: Forest Service seeks to choose commercial use areas. The Associated Press (Published: October 9, 2002) Juneau -- The U.S. Forest Service wants to designate 47 spots in Southeast Alaska for use by large commercial tour groups. The proposal covers about 5,000 miles of shoreline from South Baranof Island to Skagway. The agency recently released a draft environmental impact statement that would guide commercial recreation in the area. Public comments on the plan will be accepted through the first part of January, according to team leader Bill Tremblay. The proposal would affect outfitter-guides, smaller cruise ships, hunters, kayakers and anglers on the north end of the Tongass National Forest. The goal is to take a comprehensive look at commercial …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | UAA targets industry hot spot GRADUATES Seventeen students become first alumni of new program. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 9, 2002) The University of Alaska Anchorage recently graduated its first group of logistics master's students, part of the university's effort to offer degrees that target the hot spots in the region's economy. The 17 graduates are the vanguard of a program that is booming for the university, said Oliver Hedgepeth, the department's interim chair, and Anchorage companies said they are glad to have the opportunity available for their employees. Reviews from graduates are mixed. Logistics -- buying, storing and moving goods as efficiently as possible -- is an integral, behind-the-scenes part of commerce. It's getting apples to Barrow, sal…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outhouse was site of historic sighting SPUTNIK I: Plaque installed at place where satellite was first seen. The Associated Press (Published: November 4, 2002) Fairbanks -- The site of Dexter Stegemeyer's old outhouse now sports a plaque designating it as a historic place. Fairbanks scientists Neil Davis and Neal Brown visited the landmark off Miller Hill Road and installed the plaque in mid-October, according to the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. It all goes back to the morning of Oct. 6, 1957, when Stegemeyer was in his outhouse and all was well with the world. The door was open. As he looked up in the sky, he witnessed the dawn of a new age. "Mr. Stegemeyer said he was just sitting there enjoying the beauty of the stars twinkling in the sky when he saw a strange moving star come up out …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: October 29, 2002) Russia-based trade center sought Members of World Trade Center Alaska are pursuing development and construction of a world trade center in Yuzhno-Sakhalinsk on Russia's Sakhalin Island. The Anchorage-based group said it's looking for "letters of interest" from major international tenants that might want to lease or buy space in the office building. Sakhalin, north of Japan off Russia's east coast, is a hot new oil and gas province, with the first large field already producing, another targeting 2005 for startup and still others proposed. The trade center envisions a building with offices for businesses, consulates and associations; some retailing; a private club for tenants; and an exhibit center. More information is available from D…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Group hopes economy will take off PLAN: More activity at airport seen as a way to create jobs. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 8, 2002) With $150,000 of state money in hand and $100,000 more in private donations and staff time, the Anchorage Economic Development Corp. Wednesday outlined a globe-trotting plan to strengthen local businesses and create new jobs by boosting activity at the city's international airport. The idea is to leverage a decade of growth in Anchorage's air cargo industry by targeting shippers, cargo handlers and other related companies that could take advantage of the Anchorage hub. Over the next 11 months, AEDC head Larry Crawford said, the group will tout Anchorage's advantages to big-name companies in North America, Asia and Europe by pho…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | The fight for rights Gains made under sovereignty must be protected, Kitka says By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 25, 2002) Caroline Pewayah holds her niece Margaret Kava while visiting with friends downstairs at the Egan Center on Thursday. Pewayah and her brother Truman Kava are selling their artwork at the AFN convention. ( Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Taonui Kokako of the Nagati Maniapoto tribe performed with the Maori Dancers of New Zealand during the opening day of the AFN convention. ( Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Challenging times lie ahead for Alaska's tribes, ranging from anticipated cuts in their federal funding to challenges to their legal status. But on the opening day of their annual convention…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Glacial dam break is break for experts too THREAT: Risk to Situk River fisheries lessens as lake drains. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 16, 2002) The huge lake pent up by Hubbard Glacier's advance to the face of Gilbert Point emptied at breakneck speed when a gravelly moraine gave way to the pressure of the water. Seventy-square-mile Russell Lake crested at 61 feet above sea level at 3 a.m. Wednesday. Some 34 hours later, it had drained to 16 feet above sea level. The release also relieved the pressure -- at least for now -- on Yakutat, a community of about 800 with an economy that revolves around world-class steelhead and salmon fisheries on the Situk River. Scientists say Hubbard Glacier is on a slow advance that will eventually seal off the channel that connec…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Board backs marine area shield policy FISHERIES: The goal is to foster biodiversity, protect species. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 22, 2002) The Alaska Board of Fisheries has voted unanimously in support of drafting a policy for marine protected areas. These are swaths of ocean often closed to human activities, including commercial fishing, dredging or oil production. The goal is to foster biodiveristy in some cases or to help particular species recover from overfishing, pollution or other threats. In some of these marine reserves, all such activities may be restricted. Other areas may be recognized as a protected but have no management limitations. It's much like an underwater national park, monument or grassland, where rules and uses vary. The Alaska polic…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hells Angels sue over seized items CLUB MEMORABILIA: Complaint says officers have kept property. The Associated Press (Published: November 3, 2002) Fairbanks -- The Hells Angels are suing Alaska. A lawyer representing the Fairbanks branch of the motorcycle club filed a lawsuit against the state and the Department of Public Safety. Plaintiffs claim Alaska State Troopers and other law officers refuse to return members' vests, jackets, buttons and patches seized over the past several years. In a two-page civil complaint, attorney Bill Satterberg alleges officers have taken club memorabilia, claiming it was necessary for evidence, but then failed to return it even after cases have been resolved. In some instances, the officers have said the items were returned but they never were, the laws…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fourplex Blaze Kills 3-year-old Boy SOUTH ANCHORAGE: Early-morning Fire Spewed 15-foot Flames. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 3, 2002) Firefighters Make Way For Investigators Saturday Afternoon At The Scene Of A Fatal Fire At 7420 Nancy St. Near Brayton Drive Just East Of The Seward Highway. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News ) Click on photo to enlarge A 3-year-old boy died Saturday morning in an inferno that tore through a South Anchorage fourplex in minutes. Firefighters arrived at the apartment complex on Nancy Street to find 15-foot flames already blowing out of the apartment's windows. Neighbors woke to the sounds of the child's mother outside the structure begging for help. "I heard a lady screaming 'Get my baby! Get my baby!' and I heard a wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaskans need to go with ever-flowing humanity Craig Medred (Published: November 3, 2002) Change is a stream always flowing. We may be able to direct it, but there's no way to stop it. A dam will hold back the water for a time, but once its reservoir fills, the stream flows again. With sentiments for walling off Alaska on the rise in some circles, the inevitability of change deserves some discussion. No matter how hard today's Alaskans try, the state cannot be made an island. Alaska cannot be frozen in time no matter how much any Kenai River angler or Nelchina Basin caribou hunter might wish for that. No one can stop the immigrations that stretch far back into prehistory. None of us should forget that the guy who got here yesterday is not the first newcomer. He or she is really not muc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Earthquake rocks Interior, Southcentral Alaska By DAN JOLING, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska(November 3, 7:25 p.m. AST) - A major earthquake rocked a sparsely populated area of Interior Alaska early Sunday afternoon, cracking highways and roads, knocking over fuel tanks and shaking rural homes. Bruce Turner of the West Coast and Alaska Tsunami Warning Center in Palmer said the magnitude 7.9 quake hit at 1:13 p.m Alaska Standard Time and was centered 90 miles south of Fairbanks. "It shook for a good 30 seconds," he said. The quake was felt strongly in Anchorage about 270 miles south of the epicenter. The quake triggered the trans-Alaska pipeline's automatic detection system, said Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. spokesman Mike Heatwole. Operators then manually shut the pipeline d…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Unocal to shut Inlet platforms DOWNSIZE: Dillon to close this year, Baker early next year. The Associated Press (Published: October 31, 2002) Kenai -- Two Cook Inlet Unocal oil platforms will shut down within the next six months as the company reduces its scale. The company is taking a look at how to downsize in an effort to become more profitable, according to Unocal spokeswoman Roxanne Sinz. "We have functions in Kenai and Anchorage that are redundant," she said Tuesday. As a result of restructuring, the company will "shut in" operations at its Dillon platform by the end of the year and cease operations at the Baker platform by the close of the first quarter of 2003, Sinz said. This will directly affect six crew members working seven-day shifts on Baker and six crew members assigned …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cave experts oppose plans for timber sale KARST: Forested limestone covers cave networks that some scientists consider priceless. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 2, 2002) A group called the Tongass Cave Project has asked the U.S. Forest Service to reconsider a timber sale on Kosciusko Island in Southeast Alaska in order to protect a swatch of forested limestone known as karst. Beneath the karst is a network of caves that may hold clues about the origins of the region and its human and animal inhabitants, the group says. Valery White of the Tongass Cave Project stands in front of "Feather Cave" on Kosciusko Island. (Photo by Pete Smith / Tongass Cave Project) Click on photo to enlarge Scientists and cave experts are lobbying to protect a swath of forested limes…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | No oil tax hike, says Murkowski GOVERNOR'S RACE: GOP candidate tells Conoco Phillips he favors incentives. Know Your Candidates By Dan Joling The Associated Press (Published: October 24, 2002) Republican Frank Murkowski pledged not to raise oil company taxes if he's elected governor. Speaking Wednesday to employees of Conoco Phillips, the 22-year U.S senator replied with an emphatic "no" when asked if he would raise oil company taxes. "That will be the first thing I oppose," he said. "We want to provide incentives." Murkowski said Gov. Tony Knowles and Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer, Murkowski's Democrat opponent for governor, did not do enough to encourage development. "They put out a big sign and said, 'We're open for business,' but they did not really mean it," Murkowski said. Ulmer campaign s…

summary: | | 'External threats' worry group RESOLUTIONS: Delegates authorize effort to protect, defend rights. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 27, 2002) The Parky Parade at the Alaska Federation of Natives convention took the show on the road Saturday. After the display of traditional parkas was postponed for an hour, the models paraded around the block downtown to the appreciation of passers-by. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Responding to new congressional pressures and "external threats" to Native rights, the Alaska Federation of Natives on Saturday authorized a specially funded strategic lobbying effort "to protect and defend our political, cultural and hunting and fishing rights." The AFN convention backed a plan presented by its …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Winter kings not part of annual limit BOARD OF FISHERIES: Panel reverses decision, tightens bag limits. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 22, 2002) Homer -- The state Board of Fisheries reversed a controversial decision Monday and allowed anglers to continue catching king salmon in winter without deducting them from a five-king annual limit. Small winter king fisheries have developed in Homer, Seward and Kodiak, targeting feeder kings passing through local waters rather than local spawning runs that appear in summer. The Fish Board attempted to rein in those growing fisheries last November when it voted to begin counting winter kings as part of a five-fish annual limit for each angler. The new policy drew howls of protest from the communities. Fishermen complained …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fish summit focuses on the future By Laine Welch Freelance (Published: April 6, 2002) "If I've stepped on some toes today, it was intentional," said Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, in his final remark to a gymnasium full of rapt listeners at last week's "salmon summit" in Kodiak. As keynote speaker for the daylong event that focused on the state's ailing salmon industry, Stevens told the 40 "summiteers" and an audience of about 200 that he would do all he could to help, but he made it clear that it's up to Alaskans to get the job done. "My purpose in coming here today as your senator is to ask for your help," he said. "Your congressional delegation needs your consensus on ideas. We want to assist you on any good ideas that promote change and benefit Alaska's coastal communities." Stevens s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Salmon economic emergency declared HELP: State agencies to offer fishermen, families assistance. The Associated Press (Published: October 18, 2002) Juneau -- Gov. Tony Knowles declared a statewide economic emergency Thursday for a state salmon industry suffering from low prices. Several state agencies are being directed to offer eligible Alaska fishermen assistance in debt restructuring, job training and other programs such as Denali KidCare and energy assistance. The emergency assistance is comparable to that previously extended to Western Alaska and covers Southeast, Yakutat, Kotzebue and other salmon ports, the governor's office said. Knowles ordered the Department of Community and Economic Development to coordinate outreach to offer assistance to salmon fishing families. The action…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Tale of big bird catches some air SOUTHWEST: Letterman, radio stations have some fun with Alaska sighting. Southwest Alaskans see bird they say is Super Cub-sized Steller's sea eagle By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 18, 2002) A newspaper story this week about the sightings of a large bird in Southwest Alaska turned out to have wings. The tale of villagers seeing an eaglelike bird with a wingspan as long as a Dodge van brought dozens of e-mails from readers in Alaska and the Lower 48 and one from the Netherlands. The online newsmagazine Drudge Report posted links. The San Diego FM radio program "Smooth Jazz" talked it up. And David Letterman joked about it. John Bouker, the Dillingham pilot who saw the bird while flying into Manokotak recently and who was mentione…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Real Human Beings prevail at BizBee SPELLING: Xysti puts architects' team over Word Wizards. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 31, 2002) M. Mense Architects recaptured the crown of corporate spelling during the BizBee -- but just barely. The Real Human Beings team went head to head in final rounds with the University of Alaska Anchorage's Word Wizards, whose magic ran out on "echoppe," an engraver's needle beveled to an oval facet at the end and used to reopen previously incised lines. The winning team morphed into the Amazing Human Beings when it cinched first place with its championship word "xysti," a long, open portico used especially by ancient Greeks or Romans for athletic exercises in wintry or stormy weather.
